title: Editorial Weight Relay
prompt:
Create a production-ready Editorial Weight Relay component using React, Tailwind CSS, and Framer Motion. The visual direction is three words in one oversized black headline relaying bold weight while a fixed cyan index line anchors the composition. Keep the composition aligned with a premium light-theme SaaS dashboard: use a white or #F4F5F6 surface, #111111 primary text, #4A4A4F supporting text, 20–24px corner radii, a one-pixel #ECECEE border where separation is needed, and only an extremely soft shadow. Do not add card gradients, default blue links, heavy shadows, or decorative clutter.

The interaction must work as follows: emphasis moves word by word without reflow, and the full selectable sentence preserves one reading order. Implement motion precisely: use 420ms scale and opacity weight approximation, 80ms word stagger, and stiffness 300 damping 25. Animate transform and opacity whenever possible to avoid layout thrashing. The effect should start in a meaningful static state during server rendering, replay cleanly when remounted, and avoid infinite animation unless the pattern is explicitly a loader or ambient background. Use Framer Motion variants where multiple children coordinate, and keep state local to the component.

Make the component responsive from a 320px viewport through desktop widths. It must remain legible inside a compact preview card and expand naturally in a larger detail preview without fixed page-level positioning. Use fluid spacing, clamp oversized typography when appropriate, and keep touch targets at least 44 by 44 pixels. Do not depend on remote images, external CSS, network requests, random values, timers that are never cleaned up, or framework-specific server APIs.

Meet accessibility requirements: use semantic HTML, an explicit accessible name for icon-only controls, visible keyboard focus, logical tab order, sufficient text contrast, and appropriate ARIA only when native semantics are insufficient. Respect prefers-reduced-motion by replacing large movement with a short opacity change or a stable final state. Return a self-contained TypeScript React component that can be pasted into a Next.js client component, followed by any minimal usage example needed to reproduce the preview accurately.
